name: org.machanism.machai:gw-maven-plugin
description: GW Maven Plugin is a Java-based Maven plugin that integrates MachAI Ghostwriter into Maven builds for guided documentation and file processing. The plugin provides build-time goals that scan project trees, especially documentation folders such as src/site, detect embedded @guidance directives, and run Ghostwriter processors to generate, transform, or maintain project content. It supports both aggregator and per-module execution models for single-module and multi-module Maven projects. The available goals include gw:gw for reverse-order submodule-first guided processing similar to the Ghostwriter CLI, gw:gw-per-module for standard Maven reactor-order execution, gw:act for applying an interactive or predefined action prompt over scanned files, gw:act-per-module for execution-root action processing in a reactor build, and gw:clean for removing temporary artifacts created during processing. The plugin centralizes common configuration such as gw.model, gw.scanDir, gw.instructions, gw.excludes, logInputs, and GenAI credential resolution from Maven settings.xml via genai.serverId. It also exposes class-discovery and reflection helper tools through org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.tools so AI-assisted workflows can inspect project and dependency classes during execution. This artifact is best classified as a Maven build and documentation automation plugin for Java projects, with strong relevance to AI-assisted developer productivity, guided content maintenance, Maven reactor processing, and build-integrated Ghostwriter workflows.

usageContext: Guided scanning and transformation of project documentation
AI-assisted maintenance of files containing @guidance instructions
Interactive action-prompt execution over documentation trees
Single-module and multi-module Maven reactor processing
Credential-backed GenAI execution using Maven settings.xml
Build-integrated class metadata inspection for AI workflows

constructors.description: Creates the Maven Mojo that implements the gw:gw goal. In real usage this constructor is called by the Maven plugin runtime, not by user application code. The goal runs Ghostwriter guided file processing as an aggregator, can execute even when requiresProject is false, and processes modules in reverse order so submodules are handled before parent modules.

examples.description: How to use it: Step 1, install the plugin by declaring it in the build section of pom.xml. Step 2, Maven resolves the plugin artifact and prepares the org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.GWMojo class for execution when the gw:gw goal is invoked.

How to use it: Step 3, run the goal from the project root. Maven instantiates org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.GWMojo, applies plugin parameters, scans the selected directory, and starts guided processing with Ghostwriter.

constructors.description: Creates the Maven Mojo that implements the gw:act goal. This goal applies an interactive or predefined action prompt to scanned documents. In typical use Maven constructs the mojo automatically and the user provides the action through -Dgw.act or interactive prompting.

examples.description: How to use it: Step 1, add the plugin to pom.xml so the org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.ActMojo goal becomes available in the build environment.
How to use it: Step 2, invoke the action goal with a practical prompt and a scan directory. Maven instantiates org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.ActMojo and applies the prompt across the scanned files.

features.name: execute
features.description: Feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.GWMojo that runs the gw:gw Maven goal. This goal performs Ghostwriter-guided file processing for a project, supports execution without a pom.xml, and uses reverse-order processing so child modules are processed before parent modules. It is the main entry point for aggregator-style guided file workflows.

examples.description: Practical how to use it: Step 1, ensure the plugin is installed or invokable by coordinates. Step 2, place @guidance blocks in the files you want Ghostwriter to manage. Step 3, run mvn gw:gw from the project root. Step 4, review the generated or updated content.
Practical how to use it: Enable multithreaded module processing, restrict the scan to documentation content, and log workflow inputs for traceability in a larger Maven project.

features.name: execute
features.description: Feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.GWPerModuleMojo that runs guided processing once per module in a Maven reactor build. Maven controls execution order according to the reactor dependency graph, making this goal appropriate when standard per-module build semantics are preferred over aggregator traversal.

examples.description: Practical how to use it: Run the plugin in a multi-module build so each module is processed within Maven's standard reactor context. This is useful when module ordering should follow Maven dependency resolution.
Practical how to use it: Configure instructions, exclude generated folders, and resolve GenAI credentials from Maven settings.xml while processing modules in reactor order.

features.name: execute
features.description: Feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.ActMojo that runs the gw:act goal. This goal applies an action prompt over a documentation tree. If the gw.act parameter is missing, the plugin can prompt interactively through org.codehaus.plexus.components.interactivity.Prompter to collect multi-line input from the user.

examples.description: Practical how to use it: Use a direct prompt when you want a one-time transformation over documentation files without changing the embedded guidance rules.
Practical how to use it: Keep reusable action definitions in a directory, invoke the goal, and optionally provide the final prompt interactively when Maven asks for it.

features.name: execute
features.description: Feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.ActPerModuleMojo that runs an action prompt in an execution-root reactor context. It is a reactor-friendly alternative to the aggregator action goal and is intended for standard Maven project execution where the root project controls the scan.

examples.description: Practical how to use it: In a multi-module build, run a single action-oriented transformation from the execution root while keeping standard reactor build behavior.

features.name: getConfiguration
features.description: Shared configuration feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.AbstractGWMojo that builds the processor configuration for downstream Ghostwriter execution. If a Maven server id is configured, the method reads the matching server from settings.xml and copies username, password, and custom XML configuration values into the configurator. This is the key integration point for secure GenAI credential resolution in Maven environments.

examples.description: How to use it: Step 1, define provider credentials in ~/.m2/settings.xml. Step 2, reference them with -Dgenai.serverId=my-model-server when running a GW goal. Step 3, the plugin loads those values into the runtime configuration before Ghostwriter starts.

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Feature of org.machanism.machai.gw.maven.tools.ClassFunctionalTools that registers class-related function tools with the AI provider. These tools allow AI-assisted workflows to discover Java classes by regex and fetch structured class metadata from the current Maven project and its dependencies.

examples.description: Practical how to use it: This helper is created inside the plugin runtime so Ghostwriter processors can expose controlled classpath-inspection functions to the AI provider during guided execution.

customizations.name: gw.model
customizations.description: Configuration property that selects the provider and model identifier forwarded to the Ghostwriter workflow, for example openai:gpt-4o-mini.

examples.description: Choose the AI provider and model used during guided processing.

customizations.name: gw.scanDir
customizations.description: Configuration property that overrides the default scan root. It is commonly set to documentation trees such as src/site when the user wants to limit scanning to Maven Site sources or another focused content area.

examples.description: Restrict processing to a documentation directory instead of scanning the whole module or execution root.

customizations.name: gw.instructions
customizations.description: Configuration property that points to instruction locations consumed by the workflow. These instructions can guide how Ghostwriter interprets and processes the scanned documents.

examples.description: Provide an explicit instruction source to refine how content processing should behave.

customizations.name: gw.excludes
customizations.description: Configuration property that lists paths or patterns to skip during scanning, such as target or node_modules. This is useful to avoid generated output and irrelevant directories.

examples.description: Prevent generated directories and unrelated folders from being scanned by the workflow.

customizations.name: gw.act
customizations.description: Configuration property used by action goals to specify the prompt text applied to scanned files. If omitted for gw:act, the plugin can collect the text interactively.

examples.snippet: mvn gw:act -Dgw.act="Rewrite this documentation in a more concise style"
examples.description: Run a one-off prompt-driven transformation non-interactively from the command line.

customizations.name: gw.acts
customizations.description: Configuration property that points to a directory containing predefined action definitions used by action-oriented plugin goals.

examples.description: Resolve reusable action definitions from a dedicated folder instead of embedding every prompt directly in the command line.

customizations.name: gw.threads
customizations.description: Configuration property for the gw:gw aggregator goal that enables or disables multi-threaded module processing.

examples.description: Enable parallelized module processing when using the aggregator goal.

customizations.name: genai.serverId
customizations.description: Configuration property that selects the Maven settings.xml server entry used to resolve GenAI credentials and extra provider-specific settings.

examples.description: Load provider credentials from ~/.m2/settings.xml instead of storing them directly in the project.

customizations.name: logInputs
customizations.description: Configuration property that determines whether the plugin logs the list of input files passed to the Ghostwriter workflow.

examples.description: Enable additional execution visibility for debugging, auditing, or troubleshooting a processing run.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run guided documentation processing with the GW Maven Plugin
examples.scenario: Use the plugin as a ready-to-use Maven component for guided file processing in a Java project.
steps: Step 1: Add the GW Maven Plugin to the build/plugins section of your pom.xml.
Step 2: Place @guidance blocks in the files you want Ghostwriter to analyze or maintain.
Step 3: If your AI provider requires credentials, create a matching server entry in ~/.m2/settings.xml.
Step 4: Choose a scan root such as src/site if you want to focus on documentation.
Step 5: Run the gw:gw goal from the project root and review the resulting file changes.
examples.description: This end-to-end example shows how to install the plugin, configure optional credentials, focus processing on documentation content, and run guided Ghostwriter automation directly from Maven.

examples.title: Apply a one-off documentation rewrite using the action goal
examples.scenario: Use the plugin to run a practical prompt-driven transformation over documentation files.
steps: Step 1: Ensure the plugin is available in the build or invoke it by full coordinates.
Step 2: Decide which directory to scan, for example src/site.
Step 3: Pass a concrete action prompt with -Dgw.act, such as rewriting headings or improving clarity.
Step 4: Execute the gw:act goal and inspect the changed files before committing them.
examples.description: This scenario demonstrates how to use the action-oriented part of the plugin for practical content maintenance tasks without changing the project's embedded guidance instructions.

examples.title: Run per-module processing in a multi-module Maven reactor
examples.scenario: Use reactor-aware goals when standard Maven module ordering is required.
steps: Step 1: Open the execution-root directory of the multi-module Maven build.
Step 2: If needed, configure provider credentials in ~/.m2/settings.xml.
Step 3: Run gw:gw-per-module to process modules in Maven reactor order.
Step 4: If you need an action prompt instead of general guided processing, use gw:act-per-module.
examples.description: This example shows the recommended workflow for multi-module builds that should follow Maven reactor semantics rather than aggregator-style reverse module traversal.

examples.title: Use Maven settings.xml for secure GenAI credential resolution
examples.scenario: Configure the plugin for real-world secure execution without hardcoding secrets into the project.
steps: Step 1: Add a server entry to ~/.m2/settings.xml containing the provider credentials and optional XML configuration values.
Step 2: Reference that server id with -Dgenai.serverId when invoking the plugin.
Step 3: Run a normal guided or action goal and allow the plugin to load the credentials into the runtime configurator.
examples.description: This practical example focuses on secure plugin configuration for CI and local development by using Maven's standard settings.xml server mechanism.

examples.title: Clean temporary workflow artifacts after processing
examples.scenario: Use the plugin's maintenance goal to remove transient files created during Ghostwriter processing.
steps: Step 1: Run one or more guided or action-based GW goals that may create temporary artifacts.
Step 2: Execute mvn gw:clean from the module or project root.
Step 3: Verify that temporary workflow files have been removed.
examples.description: This example demonstrates the ready-to-use cleanup goal that keeps the working tree and module directories free of temporary GW processing artifacts.
